situation: British graphic designer applied for a German Freiberufler visa post-Brexit. Submitted bank statements showing €8,000 in savings. Embassy denied the visa citing insufficient financial reserves to cover the initial client acquisition phase without active German clients already secured.
rejection_reason: Financial proof did not demonstrate sufficient funds to sustain the applicant during settlement. The visa officer required evidence of at least 12 months of living expenses (€12,000–€15,000) plus business startup costs as a freelancer.
what_worked: Compiled a stronger application: savings of €18,000, two signed freelance contracts with UK clients continuing to pay in EUR, and a detailed business plan with projected German client revenue for months 3–6. Included a letter from a German Steuerberater confirming the business plan was viable. Visa approved on the second attempt.
what_failed: Appealing with just additional bank statements alone. Providing references from previous UK clients without contracts. Submitting a signed German rental agreement as a show of commitment.
